coredata.dc:title: Using high-energy phosphate as energy-donor and nucleus growth-inhibitor to prepare carbon dots for hydrogen peroxide related biosensing
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Na
Liu, Shi Gang
Dong, Jiang Xue
Fan, Yu Zhu
Ju, Yan Jun
Luo, Hong Qun
Li, Nian Bing
coredata.dc:description: The various synthetic routes of carbon dots (C-dots) feature a considerable step toward their potential use in sensors and biotechnology. Herein, by coupling pyrophosphate introduction with high-energy phosphate bond design, the fluorescence performance of C-dots is improved greatly. The introduction of pyrophosphate acts as the nucleus growth-inhibitor to protect C-dots from assembling and growing into large carbon particles. The high-energy phosphate bond is designed as an energy-donor to provide energy for synthesizing C-dots. The C-dots exhibit enhanced fluorescence with a high quantum yield of 24.8%. Then the C-dots are employed as the fluorescent probe to develop hydrogen peroxide (H2O2)-related biosensors. The mechanism is based on the fluorescence quenching of C-dots caused by the highly reactive OH and Fe3+ during the Fenton reaction. A wide linear range (0.5–100 μM) and a low detection limit (0.195 μM) are achieved for H2O2 detection. Moreover, the probe can be applied to H2O2-related biosensing in the presence of oxidase. As a proof-of-concept, a glucose sensor is developed with glucose oxidase. The presented glucose sensor exhibits good selectivity and high sensitivity with a detection limit of 0.254 μM. Additionally, the practical application of the proposed biosensor has been confirmed by detecting glucose in human serum samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
High-energy phosphate bond
Hydrogen peroxide
Fluorescent sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of H2O2 and glucose
Effect of pyrophosphate introduction on C-dots synthesis
The establishment of fluorescent platform for H2O2 detection
The proposed fluorescent sensor for glucose detection
Response mechanism of C-dots to Fenton reagents
The practicality of the developed sensor for glucose detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Municipal Science Foundation of Chongqing City

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21273174 , 21675131 ), the Municipal Science Foundation of Chongqing City (No. CSTC-2015jcyjB50001 ), and the Innovation Foundation of Chongqing City for Postgraduate ( CYB CYB16053 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic of the preparation route of C-dots prepared with PPi or Pi. (B) The fluorescence intensity of C-dots prepared with Pi (a) or PPi (b) with different concentration ratios of phosphorus element to adenosine. (C) The fluorescence intensity of C-dots produced from ADP (a3), ATP (b3), and adenosine with Pi or PPi added (A + 2Pi (a1), A + 1PPi (a2), A + 3Pi (b1), A + 1.5PPi (b2)). Herein, “a” and “b” represent the ratios of phosphorus element to adenosine in precursors, which are 2 and 3, respectively; “1”, “2”, and “3” are utilized to name the different samples. The concentrations of adenosine, ADP, and ATP are 10 mM. Reaction conditions: 150 °C, 2 h.
Hydrodynamic size of C-dots obtained from adenosine with (A) and without (B) PPi by dynamic light scattering. Concentrations: adenosine (10 mM), and PPi (20 mM).
(A) The TEM and (B) HRTEM image of C-dots prepared with PPi. (C) The XPS spectrum and (D) High-resolution C1s XPS spectrum of C-dots prepared with PPi.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of C-dots in the presence of Fe2+ (0.3 mM) and H2O2 (0–100 μM). (B) The plot of fluorescence intensity with different concentrations of H2O2. The inset is the linear relationship between (F 0 − F)/F and the H2O2 concentration from 0.5 to 100 μM. (C) Fluorescence response of C-dots in the presence of 0–100 μM glucose. (D) The plot of fluorescence intensity with different concentrations of glucose. The inset is the linear relationship between (F 0 − F)/F and the glucose concentration from 0.5 to 100 μM. Herein, F and F 0 respectively represent the fluorescence intensity of C-dots in the presence and absence of target molecule.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of (a) C-dots, (b) C-dots + 0.3 mM Fe2+ + 100 μM H2O2, (c) C-dots + 0.3 mM Fe2+, (d) C-dots + 100 μM H2O2, and (e) C-dots + 0.3 mM Fe3+. (B) Fluorescence spectra of (a) C-dots, (b) C-dots + 0.3 mM Fe2+ + 100 μM H2O2, (c) C-dots + 0.3 mM Fe2+ + 100 μM H2O2 + 2 mM NaF, (d) C-dots + 0.3 mM Fe2+ + 100 μM H2O2 + 0.5 mM thiourea. (C) UV-vis absorption spectum of Fe3+ and fluorescence excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of C-dots. (D) Decay curves of C-dots in the absence (a) and presence (b) of 0.3 mM Fe2+ and 50 μM H2O2. λex = 350 nm and λem = 420 nm.

(A) The UV-vis absorption spectra of the resulting solution in the presence of different concentrations of glucose (GOD-POD method). (B) The plot of absorbance of the resulting solution with different concentrations of glucose (GOD-POD method). The inset is the linear relationship between absorbance and the glucose concentration (0, 10, 20, 40, 60, 100, 200, 300, 400, and 600 μM). (C) The comparison of the proposed method with GOD-POD approach for glucose sensing in diluted human serum samples.

head.ce:title: Using high-energy phosphate as energy-donor and nucleus growth-inhibitor to prepare carbon dots for hydrogen peroxide related biosensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-Environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Eco-Environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education)
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The various synthetic routes of carbon dots (C-dots) feature a considerable step toward their potential use in sensors and biotechnology. Herein, by coupling pyrophosphate introduction with high-energy phosphate bond design, the fluorescence performance of C-dots is improved greatly. The introduction of pyrophosphate acts as the nucleus growth-inhibitor to protect C-dots from assembling and growing into large carbon particles. The high-energy phosphate bond is designed as an energy-donor to provide energy for synthesizing C-dots. The C-dots exhibit enhanced fluorescence with a high quantum yield of 24.8%. Then the C-dots are employed as the fluorescent probe to develop hydrogen peroxide (H2O2)-related biosensors. The mechanism is based on the fluorescence quenching of C-dots caused by the highly reactive OH and Fe3+ during the Fenton reaction. A wide linear range (0.5–100 μM) and a low detection limit (0.195 μM) are achieved for H2O2 detection. Moreover, the probe can be applied to H2O2-related biosensing in the presence of oxidase. As a proof-of-concept, a glucose sensor is developed with glucose oxidase. The presented glucose sensor exhibits good selectivity and high sensitivity with a detection limit of 0.254 μM. Additionally, the practical application of the proposed biosensor has been confirmed by detecting glucose in human serum samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon-based materials have been the focus of scientists for use as probes in chemical sensors and biological applications [1,2]. For instance, fluorescent graphene have been widely used due to their large optical absorptivity, high photostability, and low toxicity [3,4]. Fluorescent carbon nanotubes have also been developed with strong fluorescence and low autofluorescence for bioimaging in vivo [5]. Recently, the rising carbon dots (C-dots) have attracted intensive research because of their superior fluorescence properties, tunable surface functionalities, well-defined shapes, and excellent biocompatibility, which distinguish them from traditional fluorescent materials [6–8]. Despite the exceptional advantages we have listed above, unfortunately, some scientific issues with C-dots still need further investigation. Since their serendipitous discovery by Scrivens et al. [9], C-dots have been prepared through a multitude of synthetic routes from polymers, molecules, and even carbogenic particles [10–12], but efficient one-step methods to obtain C-dots with high quantum yields are still challenging in this field.

Fortunately, in recent years two major approaches of surface passivation (namely, doping with heteroatoms, and surface functionalization) have been reported to obtain fluorescence enhancement [13–15]. By introducing heteroatoms (e.g., sulfur [16], phosphorus [17], nitrogen [18,19], etc.) into C-dots, doping becomes at the center of research effort due to the improved quantum yield of C-dots. Meanwhile, surface functionalization arises as an effective pathway to achieve tailored properties of C-dots [20]. To the best of our knowledge, the important role of high-energy phosphate as energy-donor and nucleus growth-inhibitor was rarely discussed for high quantum yield. Moreover, it becomes increasingly important to expand C-dots to novel applications.

Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is an important metabolite involved in diverse biological processes, such as cellular signal transduction, host defense, and biosynthesis [21,22]. Through the incomplete reduction of oxygen, H2O2 can be produced in numerous enzymatic reactions. As one of the most well-known examples, the oxidation reaction of glucose can be catalyzed by glucose oxidase (GOD) to release the byproduct H2O2. With metal ion (such as Fe2+ and Cu+) as a catalyst, excess H2O2 can cause the Fenton reaction, which results in the generation of hydroxyl radicals (OH) [23,24]. Elevated levels of highly reactive OH often link to oxidative stress, resulting in a raised risk of cellular disorders. For these reasons, it becomes increasingly important to develop reliable H2O2 sensing methods. To date, a number of techniques have been reported for H2O2 detection, such as chromatography [25], chemiluminescence [26], fluorometry [27–29], and electrochemistry [30,31]. Among them, fluorescence methods have attracted much attention because of the simple operation, cost-effective instrumentation, and requisite sensitivity and selectivity. Recently, Liu and co-workers designed an H2O2 sensor by displacing DNA from nanoceria [27]. Chen et al. reported a new fluorescent probe lighted up by H2O2 in vivo [32]. By employing H2O2 as an intermediate, Liu et al. developed a switch-off fluorescent probe for H2O2-related detection based on the inner filter effect [29]. Among these fluorescence methods, the synthesis of the high-quality fluorescent probe is crucial.

Herein, we report a new way to obtain enhanced fluorescence performance of C-dots by introducing high-energy phosphate bond as an energy-donor and nucleus growth-inhibitor. Highly fluorescent C-dots were produced through hydrothermal carbonization of adenosine with pyrophosphate (PPi) solution. We demonstrate that rational PPi introduction and high-energy phosphate bond design can be integrated to improve the C-dot formation. The single-step synthetic procedure is simple, fast, and costless. The basic optical properties and morphology of C-dots have been investigated. Thus, in this work, we not only have reported a new kind of C-dots but also provide a rational strategy to produce highly fluorescent C-dots. Considering that H2O2 (when coupled with Fe2+) can induce fluorescence quenching of the C-dots, a fluorescent biosensor was developed to detect H2O2 and glucose with high sensitivity and selectivity.

For preparing C-dots with PPi, adenosine (20 mM, 2.5 mL) was mixed with PPi (40 mM, 2.5 mL) at the ratio of 1:2. Thereafter, the solution was transferred in a Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave. After hydrothermal treatment at 150 °C for 2 h, the pale yellow C-dot solution was obtained. The C-dot solution was dialyzed against water through a 1000 MWCO dialysis film for 48 h to remove excess reagents. The resulting solution (10 mL) was collected and stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator before use. In addition, the C-dots prepared with phosphate (Pi) were synthesized by mixing adenosine (10 mM) with Pi (40 mM). And then the above mixture was treated with the same procedures as the C-dots with PPi.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of H2O2 and glucose
ce:section.ce:para: For H2O2 sensing, 50 μL of C-dots (30%, v/v) and 50 μL of Fe2+ (3 mM) were mixed with 390 μL of acetate buffer (pH 4.8). Then 10 μL of H2O2 with different concentrations was added to the above solution. After thorough shaking, the mixture was incubated at room temperature for 5 min. For glucose sensing, 50 μL of C-dots (30%, v/v), 50 μL of Fe2+ (3 mM), and 10 μL of GOD (10 U/mL) were mixed with 380 μL of acetate buffer (pH 4.8). Then, 10 μL of glucose (Glu) with different concentrations was added to the above solution and the mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 25 min. Finally, the fluorescence spectra of all the samples were measured at the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of 350 and 420 nm, respectively. Error bars in the whole work represent the standard deviation of three parallel measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pyrophosphate introduction on C-dots synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: A simple hydrothermal method was utilized to produce the C-dots with PPi. As shown in Fig. 1 A, fluorescent C-dots were obtained through a one-step thermal decomposition of adenosine with PPi added. In this route, adenosine and PPi served as the molecular precursors with adenosine providing the carbon and nitrogen source and PPi as the stabilizer. The pale yellow C-dot solution was formed after heating the molecular precursor at 150 °C for 2 h. To investigate how PPi affected the C-does synthesis process, a series of experiments were performed and the results are exhibited in Fig. 1B. Interestingly, the addition of PPi greatly benefits the formation of fluorescent C-dots. Meanwhile, the fluorescence spectra and intensities of the C-dots were recorded and are shown in Fig. S1A, B in Supplementary data. In the absence of PPi, very weak fluorescence of the C-dot solution is observed (sample a, the inset of Fig. S1B). The corresponding quantum yield of C-dots prepared without PPi was determined to be 9.9% in water relative to quinine sulfate, and the detailed method and relevant data (Fig. S2) are shown in the Supplementary data. With the increase of PPi concentration, the fluorescence of C-dots is gradually enhanced and then decreased slightly. The quantum yield of C-dots produced with PPi was determined to be 24.8%, which is highly increased when compared to that of C-dots prepared without PPi. Moreover, the quantum yield of C-dots prepared with PPi in this work is comparable to that of some other carbon dots [16,33,34]. As previously reported [35], hydrothermally synthesized C-dots were generally produced through the following phases: dehydration, polymerization, carbonization, and passivation. When PPi is incubated with adenosine in aqueous solution, PPi can easily hydrolyze to produce large amounts of Pi. The electrostatic interaction can occur between an electropositive N7 atom of adenosine and electronegative Pi. During the formation process, Pi effectively stabilizes carbon nuclei and prevent them from assembling and growing into large carbon particles [18]. Correspondingly, with increasing addition of PPi, the black precipitate at the bottom of the obtained C-dot solution is greatly decreased. As a result, highly fluorescent C-dots were achieved with PPi addition. Interestingly, the C-dots prepared with Pi show the similar trend with that of PPi (Fig. S1C, D), which elucidates the protective effect of Pi. Moreover, the hydrodynamic diameter of C-dots was measured and is shown in Fig. 2 . The C-dots produced from adenosine with PPi show the hydrodynamic diameter of 10 nm, which is smaller than the diameter of C-dots prepared without PPi (17.5 nm). This result further confirms that pyrophosphate can act as the stabilizer to prevent them from assembling and growing into large carbon particles. Additionally, the zeta potentials of C-dots were measured and respectively turned out to be −5.50 and −15.7 mV in the absence and presence of PPi. The results verify the improved stability of C-dots prepared with PPi.

Meanwhile, the C-dots synthesized with PPi show stronger fluorescence than that with Pi when the ratio of phosphorus to adenosine concentration is kept the same (Fig. 1B). We speculate that the fluorescence difference is caused by the high-energy phosphate bond in PPi. High-energy phosphate bonds are phosphorus acid anhydride linkages that are produced through dehydrating the phosphoric acid derivatives. Large amounts of energy will be released during the hydrolysis process of these bonds. Generally, high-energy phosphate bonds are defined when the released energy is above 20.92 kJ/mol [36]. The PPi aqueous solution is stable below 70 °C, and is hydrolyzed to Pi when heated in boiling water [37–39]. When PPi is incubated with adenosine at 150 °C for 2 h during the hydrothermal process, the high-energy phosphate bond of PPi will be broken and hydrolyzed. The released energy is so high that it can render energy for synthesizing C-dots. To verify that the presence of high-energy phosphate bond is conducive to C-dots formation, adenosine diphosphate (ADP) and adenosine triphosphate (ATP) are separately utilized as the precursor to prepare C-dots. As can be seen in Table S1 [40,41], ADP and ATP can release high energy when hydrolyzed, which is comparable with that from PPi. The fluorescence properties of C-dots produced from ADP or ATP were explored and are shown in Fig. 1C. The fluorescence of the C-dots from the precursor containing high-energy phosphate bond is very strong, suggesting the important role of high-energy phosphate bond. In addition, C-dots were synthesized under raised reaction temperature or prolonged heating time to explain the benefit of high energy to producing C-dots. As illustrated in Fig. S3, the fluorescence of C-dots is enhanced when the reaction temperature or the heating time is increased. In general, the introduction of pyrophosphate with high-energy phosphate bond is conducive to the production of high fluorescent C-dots.

To investigate the optical properties and morphology of C-dots, C-dots prepared with PPi were taken as representatives and characterized. As exhibited in Fig. S4A, the C-dot solution shows strong absorption at about 260 nm, which is because of the π → π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains [42]. Additionally, a weak broad peak appears at around 340 nm, which can be attributed to the n → π* transition of the CO bond [43]. The obtained C-dot solution is pale yellow and emits strong blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (Fig. S4A, inset). The optical study also reveals the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of 350 and 420 nm, respectively. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded under different excitation wavelengths (Fig. S4B). There is no obvious shift in the emission peak with changed excitation wavelengths from 330 to 390 nm, which is probably due to the narrow size distribution of C-dots and the effect of surface passivation functionality [43,44].

The morphology of C-dots prepared with PPi was investigated and is shown in Fig. 3 A, B. The TEM image shows the discrete and quasispherical nanoparticles with an average size around 2 nm, which is smaller than the size of hydrodynamic diameter. The difference is mainly due to the presence of hydration layer surrounding the C-dots during the hydrodynamic diameter measurement [45]. The lattice spacing observed in the HRTEM image is 0.21 nm, consistent with the (100) facet of graphite [46]. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to confirm the elements on C-dots synthesized with PPi. The main composition turns out to be carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and phosphorus (Fig. 3C and Table S2). The high resolution C1s spectrum exhibits the peaks centered at 287.2, 285.9, 285.1, and 284.3 eV (Fig. 3D), which can be ascribed to CO, CN, CP, and CC, respectively [17]. Additionally, the Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was recorded to explore possible functional groups on C-dots. As exhibited in Fig. S5, the absorption at 3393 and 1000 cm−1 signifies the vibration of OH bond. The sharp peak at 1643 cm−1 is attributed to CO bond stretching [47]. The asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of COC is located at 1335 and 1150 cm−1, respectively [48]. The CN absorption band at 1335 cm−1 and the NH2 stretching mode at 3393 cm−1 are seen in FTIR spectrum [49]. In conclusion, PPi stabilized C-dots with strong blue fluorescence were successfully synthesized after the hydrothermal treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The establishment of fluorescent platform for H2O2 detection
ce:section.ce:para: The C-dots prepared with PPi are highly fluorescent, water-soluble, and rich in hydroxyl and amine groups, which provide C-dots a potential route to biosensing. In the presence of Fe2+, the fluorescence of C-dots was gradually quenched with increasing H2O2 concentration, thus, an H2O2 sensor was developed. To optimize this H2O2 detection method, we investigated the effect of C-dot concentration, Fe2+ concentration, reaction time, and pH value on the fluorescence-quenched efficiency (ΔF/F 0). Herein, ΔF = F 0 − F; F and F 0 respectively represent the fluorescence intensity of C-dots in the presence and absence of target molecule. The detailed optimization results and the corresponding Fig. S6 are shown in the Supplementary data.

The H2O2 sensing system was developed under optimal conditions. After the addition of H2O2 with different concentrations, the fluorescence emission spectra of C-dots were recorded and are shown in Fig. 4 A. The fluorescence emission intensity is gradually decreased with increasing H2O2 concentration, and the maximum emission wavelength does not shift obviously. The correlation between the fluorescence intensity of C-dots and the H2O2 concentration was plotted (Fig. 4B). As was reported, the quenching of fluorescence can be described by the Stern–Volmer equation, F 0/F = 1 + K sv[Q]. In this equation, K sv is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant, [Q] is the concentration of quencher, and F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of quencher, respectively. The insets of Fig. 4B show that the fluorescence intensity ratio (F 0 − F)/F (i.e., F 0/F − 1) is linearly dependent upon the H2O2 concentration in the range of 0.5–20 μM and 20–100 μM, where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots in the absence and presence of target. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 0.195 μM based on a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, and the detailed description and corresponding data (Table S3) are exhibited in the Supplementary data. In addition, the linear range and LOD of the proposed method and some published approaches are listed in Table S4. Compared to various reported methods for H2O2 determination, the developed biosensor shows high sensitivity as well as good linear range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The proposed fluorescent sensor for glucose detection
ce:section.ce:para: By taking H2O2 as an intermediate, the fluorescent sensor can be developed for H2O2-related biosensing application. As a proof-of-concept, Glu was detected coupled with GOD. In the presence of O2, glucose can be specifically catalyzed by GOD, giving rise to the release of H2O2. When glucose and GOD are incubated with C-dots and Fe2+ solution, the obvious quenching of C-dots is seen in Fig. S7 (curve e). In contrast, the sole addition of glucose or GOD has little influence on the fluorescence of C-dots (curves c and d). To obtain optimal glucose sensing conditions, the reaction time and GOD concentration were explored and the results are shown in Fig. S8. The reaction time of 25 min and 0.2 U/mL GOD were eventually chosen for the experiment. With increased glucose concentration (from 0 to 100 μM), the fluorescence of C-dots is gradually quenched (Fig. 4C). In the wide range of 0.5–20 and 20–100 μM, the calibration curves were achieved by plotting the fluorescence intensity ratio of (F 0 − F)/F versus glucose concentrations (Fig. 4D, inset). The LOD was estimated to be 0.254 μM based on a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, which is comparable to the glucose sensors developed with some other approaches (Table S5).

To ensure that this glucose sensor can be applied to real samples, the selectivity study was performed in this experiment. The fluorescence of C-dots is greatly quenched only by the addition of glucose (Fig. S9A). As shown in Fig. S9B, the glucose-induced fluorescence-quenched efficiency is much higher than those of some other carbohydrates and metal ions (fructose (Fru), cellulose (Cel), lactose (Lac), maltose (Mal), sucrose (Suc), mannose (Man), Na+, K+, Mg2+, Zn2+, and Ca2+). These figures imply the good selectivity of the developed sensor, which shows the great potential of C-dots to complicated biosensing applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response mechanism of C-dots to Fenton reagents
ce:section.ce:para:
As exhibited in Fig. 5 A, the C-dot solution shows strong fluorescence emission (curve a). However, when the Fenton reagent (a solution of H2O2 with Fe2+ as a catalyst) is added to the C-dot solution, the fluorescence of the C-dots is greatly quenched (curve b). In contrast, H2O2 or Fe2+ alone does not induce obvious fluorescence quenching behavior (curves c, d). In addition, the effect of Fe3+ on the fluorescence of C-dots was investigated. The great fluorescence quenching is seen with the addition of Fe3+ (curve e). As is reported, the Fenton reaction works as Eq. (1).
(1) H2O2 + Fe2+ → OH + Fe3+ + OH−
The fluorescence of C-dots is probably quenched by both the highly reactive OH and Fe3+ because of their appearance during the Fenton reaction.

To further shed light on the inferred quenching mechanism, a series of experiments were performed. Firstly, masking reagents (F− for Fe3+ and thiourea for OH) were added to the C-dot solution prior to the Fenton reagents [50]. As shown in Fig. 5B, when F− is employed to mask the Fe3+ from the Fenton reaction, the fluorescence quenching is suppressed partly and about 23% recovery is achieved (curve c). However, when F− is utilized to mask the additional Fe3+ from Fe2(SO4)3, the fluorescence quenching was almost suppressed completely, giving rise to a 96% recovery (Fig. S10A). These results demonstrate that the fluorescence quenching caused by the Fenton reagents can be only partly attributed to the generated Fe3+. The synthesized C-dots have abundant surface functional groups with which Fe3+ can form a complex, indicating the possible quenching mechanism of static quenching effect. In addition, the absorption spectrum of Fe3+ shows an overlap with the excitation spectrum of C-dots (Fig. 5C), which provides the possibility of inner-filter effect between Fe3+ and C-dots. Thus, Fe3+-induced fluorescence quenching is presumably ascribed to static quenching combined by the inner-filter effect. To explore the other possible reasons for the fluorescence quenching, thiourea was employed to scavenge the OH produced during the Fenton reaction. In the presence of thiourea, the fluorescence of C-dots is conspicuously increased with 67% recovery (Fig. 5B, curve d). In control experiments, the sole addition of F− or thiourea almost has no effect on the fluorescence of C-dots (Fig. S10B). Considering that the fluorescence of C-dots is significantly affected by their surface states, the OH-induced fluorescence quenching can be ascribed to the oxidation of reductive groups by hydroxyl radical [47]. Thus, the fluorescence quenching is mainly due to the OH as well as Fe3+ produced during the Fenton reaction.

Furthermore, some relevant calculation and experiments were conducted to validate the possible mechanism proposed above. Generally speaking, fluorescence quenching usually results from static quenching effect (SQE) or dynamic quenching effect (DQE) or both of them. In the case of SQE, the nonfluorescent complex is formed by the reaction of a ground-state fluorophore with the quencher, resulting in fluorescence quenching. In DQE, the excited-state fluorophore interacts with the quencher and can be nonradiatively deactivated upon collision. Importantly, both SQE and DQE can result in the linear Stern–Volmer plot. The Stern–Volmer equation has been described above. As shown in the inset of Fig. 4B, the fluorescence intensity ratio (that is, F 0/F) exhibits a linear relationship with the H2O2 concentration (F 0/F = 0.0469[H2O2] − 0.028). The Stern–Volmer quenching constant (K sv) is obtained as 0.0469 μM−1 from the slope of the line in Fig. 4B.

The fluorescence quenching can be explained by either SQE or DQE only through the observation of a linear Stern-Volmer plot, and thus additional information is needed. As the fluorescence lifetime is an important parameter to distinguish static and dynamic quenching, the time-resolved fluorescence was recorded and is provided in Fig. 5D. For SQE, fluorescence lifetime keeps constant with the addition of the quencher. In contrast, for DQE the lifetime changes with the fluorescence quenching. As can be seen in Fig. 5D, the fluorescence decay curve of C-dots obviously overlaps with that of C-dots and H2O2. The fluorescence lifetime of C-dots is 3.53 ns before the addition of H2O2. There is only a very small change in fluorescence lifetime when H2O2 is added (3.50 ns), which proves that the fluorescence quenching is mainly caused by SQE. In addition, the DQE was investigated to further verify the above results. The Stern-Volmer quenching constant for DQE is given by K sv = k qτ0, in which τ0 is the lifetime of the fluorophore without the quencher and k q is the molecular quenching rate constant. In this experiment, k q was calculated to be 1.30 × 1013 M−1 s−1 with K sv = 0.046 μM−1 and τ0 = 3.53 ns. However, in a diffusion-controlled process in aqueous solution, the largest value of k q is considered to be near 1.0 × 1010 M−1 s−1. The obtained k q in this work is unreasonably high, suggesting that DQE is not responsible for the fluorescence quenching. In conclusion, SQE is considered as the main mechanism for the fluorescence quenching process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The practicality of the developed sensor for glucose detection
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the feasibility of proposed glucose sensor, the glucose content in human serum samples was estimated based on the developed method and referenced glucose oxidase-peroxidase (GOD-POD) method. The GOD-POD approach is commonly used in the clinical analysis, and the detailed detection procedures are shown in the Supplementary data. The linear relationship between the absorbance and the glucose concentration was investigated and is displayed in Fig. 6 . Then, the glucose contents in diluted human serum samples were detected using the two methods for comparison. As shown in Fig. 6C, the results obtained from the proposed method are consistent with that from the GOD-POD technique, which demonstrates the feasibility of the proposed method for glucose detection in the complex sample matrix.

In this work, we reported that the introduction of energy-rich phosphate compound greatly benefited the C-dots synthesis. By taking pyrophosphate as an example, the important role of high-energy phosphate bond was discussed in detail. When the high-energy phosphate bond hydrolyzed, it can not only release large amounts of energy for synthesis of C-dots but also produce the phosphate that can prevent C-dots from assembling and growing into large particles. The introduction of energy-rich phosphate compound can provide a universal method for C-dots synthesis. The produced C-dots exhibited strong fluorescence with a high quantum yield of 24.8%. As the fluorescence can be quenched by H2O2 coupled with Fe2+, the C-dots were applied to H2O2-related bioanalysis. The fluorescence quenching mechanism is mainly ascribed to the static quenching caused by OH and Fe3+ produced during the Fenton reaction. Through the real sample investigation, the proposed fluorescent sensor demonstrated the applicability for glucose monitoring in clinical analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21273174, 21675131), the Municipal Science Foundation of Chongqing City (No. CSTC-2015jcyjB50001), and the Innovation Foundation of Chongqing City for Postgraduate (CYB CYB16053).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.02.051.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoluminescence properties of graphene versus other carbon nanomaterials

Graphene oxide as a chemically tunable platform for optical applications
A route to brightly fluorescent carbon nanotubes for near-infrared imaging in mice
Glowing graphene quantum dots and carbon dots: properties syntheses and biological applications
Intraparticle FRET of Mn[ii]-doped carbon dots and its application in discrimination of volatile organic compounds
Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
Recent advances in carbon nanodots: synthesis properties and biomedical applications
Carbon quantum dots displaying dual-wavelength photoluminescence and electrochemiluminescence prepared by high-energy ball milling
A Large-scale synthesis of photoluminescent carbon quantum dots: a self-exothermic reaction driving the formation of the nanocrystalline core at room temperature
Heteroatom-doped carbon dots: synthesis characterization properties photoluminescence mechanism and biological applications
Carbon dots doped with heteroatoms for fluorescent bioimaging: a review
Improving the functionality of carbon nanodots: doping and surface functionalization
Highly luminescent s-doped carbon dots for the selective detection of ammonia
Phosphorus and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots as a fluorescent probe for real-time measurement of reactive oxygen and nitrogen species inside macrophages
Amino-functionalized green fluorescent carbon dots as surface energy transfer biosensors for hyaluronidase

Carbon-dot-based ratiometric fluorescent probe for imaging and biosensing of superoxide anion in live cells
Hydrogen peroxide: a metabolic by-product or a common mediator of ageing signals
H2O2, a necessary evil for cell signaling
LXXIII—Oxidation of tartaric acid in presence of iron
Degradation of atrazine by Fentons reagent-condition optimization and product quantification
Sensitive reversed-phase liquid chromatographic determination of hydrogen peroxide and glucose based on ternary vanadium[V]-hydrogen peroxide-2-[5-bromo-2-pyridylazo]-5-diethyl- aminophenol system
Hydroxyl radical-induced apoptosis in human tumor cells is associated with telomere shortening but not telomerase inhibition and caspase activation
Hydrogen peroxide displacing DNA from nanoceria: mechanism and detection of glucose in serum
Smart composite reagent composed of double-stranded DNA-templated copper nanoparticle and sybr green I for hydrogen peroxide related biosensing
Ratiometric fluorescent biosensing of hydrogen peroxide and hydroxyl radical in living cells with lysozyme-silver nanoclusters: lysozyme as stabilizing ligand and fluorescence signal unit
Gold-coated silica-fiber hybrid materials for application in a novel hydrogen peroxide biosensor
Graphene/intermetallic ptpb nanoplates composites for boosting electrochemical detection of H2O2 released from cells
A fluorescent probe for hydrogen peroxide in vivo based on the modulation of intramolecular charge transfer
Graphene quantum dots as universal fluorophores and their use in revealing regulated trafficking of insulin receptors in adipocytes
One-step synthesis of amino-functionalized fluorescent carbon nanoparticles by hydrothermal carbonization of chitosan
Synthesis of high-quality carbon nanodots from hydrophilic compounds: role of functional groups
Metabolic generation and utilization of phosphate bond energy
Assay of inorganic phosphate, total phosphate, and phosphatases
A kinetic study of the hydrolysis of pyrophosphates
Real-time assay of inorganic pyrophosphatase using a high-affinitychelation-enhanced fluorescence chemosensor
One-pot synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from orange waste peels
Multifunctional N,S co-doped carbon quantum dots with pH- and thermo-dependent switchable fluorescent properties and highly selective detection of glutathione
Intrinsically fluorescent nitrogen-containing carbon nanoparticles synthesized by a hydrothermal process
Water-soluble nonconjugated polymer nanoparticles with strong fluorescence emission for selective and sensitive detection of nitro-explosive picric acid in aqueous medium
Carbon-based dots co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation-independent emission
High fluorescence S, N co-doped carbon dots as an ultra-sensitive fluorescent probe for the determination of uric acid
Synthesis functionalization and bioimaging applications of highly fluorescent carbon nanoparticles
Bright-yellow-emissive n-doped carbon dots: preparation cellular imaging and bifunctional sensing
Radical cations in the oh-radical-induced oxidation of thiourea acid tetramethylthiourea in aqueous solution

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Na Li is studying for her Ph.D. degree at present in Southwest University, Chongqing, P.R. China. Her main research interests are chemical sensors and biosensors.

Shi Gang Liu is currently a Ph.D student of analytical chemistry at Southwest University, Chongqing, P.R. China. His current research interest involves the synthesis and application of fluorescent sensors.
Jiang Xue Dong is a Ph.D. student of Southwest University, Chongqing, P.R. China. Her major research interest is spectrum analysis.
Yu Zhu Fan is studying for her Ph.D. degree at present in Southwest University, Chongqing, P.R. China. Her major research interest is the application of fluorescent sensors.
Yan Jun Ju is a MS candidate in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. Her main research interests are chemical sensors and biosensors.

Hong Qun Luo is a professor of chemistry in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. She received her MS degree in environmental chemistry from Sichuan University in 1991 and PhD degree in analytical chemistry from Southwest China Normal University in 2002. During 2006–2007, she was a visiting scholar in Tohoku University, Japan. Her research is focused on molecular spectroscopy and electrochemical sensor.

Nian Bing Li is a professor of chemistry in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. He received his MS degree in physical chemistry in 1997 and PhD degree in material science in 2000 from Chongqing University. During 2000–2002, he was a postdoctoral research fellow in Fuzhou University, China. Since 2006–2007, he was a postdoctoral research fellow in Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST), Korea. His research interests are the developments of electrochemical devices such as chemical sensors and biosensors.
